speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're getting practical about code review: how to work with pull requests in VS Code, how to read diffs accessibly, how to leave comments that actually help, and how to decide whether to approve, request changes, or just comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not only, "What is a pull request?" It's also, "How do I manage one, review one, and respond like a good collaborator?"

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And for our blind and low-vision learners, there's a second thread running through everything: how to do this with a screen reader in a way that is efficient, not exhausting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I appreciate that. A visual diff can be overwhelming fast.

speaker: ALEX
text: It can. So we'll keep returning to the workflows that reduce noise, especially the GitHub Pull Requests and Issues extension in VS Code, GitHub's web review tools, and the Accessible Diff Viewer in VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: A quick setup note before we dive in. This material assumes you've already practiced basic pull request workflows and Git in VS Code. Also, whenever we mention Windows-style shortcuts like Control, Mac users should translate them: Control becomes Command, Alt becomes Option, and Shift+F10 context menu behavior is typically Control+Return on Mac.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are some related references in the background too, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you need them, a VS Code reference appendix and a Markdown reference appendix support this lesson. And the main source guidance behind today's material comes from GitHub Docs on reviewing pull requests and GitHub's accessibility guidance for pull request reviews.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Nice. So what's the big picture for the day?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three parts. First, using the GitHub Pull Requests extension in VS Code. Second, accessible code review in both the browser and VS Code. Third, the reviewer's craft: what to look for, how to write constructive feedback, and how to use judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there were workshop tasks tied to this too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Two guided practice items for the extension portion: install the extension and sign in, then check out a classmate's pull request and post one constructive comment. No automation check for those, because extension state and review state live on your local machine and your account. Evidence is usually a comment confirming what you completed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with the extension itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: The GitHub Pull Requests and Issues extension brings GitHub workflow directly into VS Code. Instead of constantly switching to a browser tab, you can see open pull requests, inspect changes, check out a PR branch, comment, create PRs, and sometimes merge, all from the editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds convenient, but I want the concrete steps. If I'm starting from zero, how do I install it?

speaker: ALEX
text: You have two main methods. The first is through the Extensions sidebar. Press Control+Shift+X. The search box in the Marketplace usually gets focus. Type GitHub Pull Requests, press Enter, move through the results, choose the extension published by GitHub, and activate Install.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone prefers the Command Palette?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's method two. Open the Command Palette with Control+Shift+P, search for extension-related commands or directly search for the GitHub Pull Requests extension, then install it from there. The sidebar method is usually simpler for most learners, but both are valid.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What happens after install?

speaker: ALEX
text: Often VS Code prompts you to sign in to GitHub. Activate Sign in to GitHub, which opens a browser window for OAuth authorization. Complete the approval in the browser, then return to VS Code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do I know it worked?

speaker: ALEX
text: Verify sign-in by going to the Explorer sidebar with Control+Shift+E. You should find a GitHub section that includes pull requests and issues. That's one of the clearest signs the extension is active and authenticated.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if a notification appeared and disappeared too fast for the screen reader?

speaker: ALEX
text: Great accessibility detail. Open the Command Palette and run Notifications: Focus Notification Toast. That brings the latest toast into focus so you can read what happened.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the extension adds a GitHub area in the interface, commands for PR work, and tighter connection between local Git and GitHub's review system.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It adds views, commands, PR trees, changed file access, comment features, checkout options, PR creation tools, and integration points with review and merge workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's say the extension is installed. How do I actually view pull requests?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are two common entry points. One is the GitHub Pull Requests panel from the Activity Bar. The other is a GitHub section in Explorer. In practice, many screen reader users like using the Command Palette to jump directly into the Pull Requests view by running GitHub Pull Requests: Focus on Pull Requests View.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once I'm there, what can I do?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can list open pull requests, move through them, open one to inspect its details, open it in your browser if needed, and filter the list so you only hear the PRs that matter to you, like items awaiting your review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's slow down on filtering. Why is that important?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because long PR lists are noisy. Filtering by status helps you narrow to open, closed, or review-requested items. Filtering by repository matters if you're connected to multiple repos. And a specific filter for PRs waiting for your review is especially useful when you're acting as reviewer rather than author.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the PR list sound like for a screen reader user?

speaker: ALEX
text: Typically you're navigating a tree or list structure. A pull request entry may announce the PR title, number, repository context, branch information, and status indicators. Inside a specific PR tree, you may find sections like description and changed files. The key idea is orientation: understand what level of the tree you're on and what object currently has focus.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So description, changed files, and maybe actions are all nested under that PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And a good screen reader announcement example might sound like a tree item with the PR title and state, then child items underneath for description or files. The exact wording varies by VS Code version and screen reader, but the structural pattern matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I want full details for one PR, what should I expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to open a detail view that includes the title, author, status, branch relationship, description, and the list of changed files. For many users, that's enough to decide whether to inspect the diff in VS Code or jump to the browser for a more familiar review form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the next big skill: checking out a PR branch. Why would I do that instead of just reading the pull request online?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because checking out a PR makes the proposed changes local. That means you can test them, run the project, inspect files with your normal editor setup, and review the diff in context. It's especially useful if you need more than a surface-level read.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's not just reading. It's validating.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You might want to verify the change actually works, that it only changes what it claims, or that it doesn't break something nearby.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do I check out a PR?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three common methods. First, from the PR detail view, activate Checkout. Second, from the PR tree itself, use the context menu on the PR item and choose checkout. Third, use the Command Palette and search for a GitHub Pull Requests checkout command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once I do that, VS Code switches me to that PR's branch?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Your local workspace now reflects that pull request branch. It's smart to notice the branch indicator in the status bar or Source Control area so you know you're no longer on your original branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That brings up an anxious question learners often have: how do I get back?

speaker: ALEX
text: Important question. Return to your original branch using the branch switcher in VS Code, the Source Control UI, or Git commands if you're comfortable with them. After a review, many people switch back to main or to their own feature branch. The key is situational awareness: always know what branch you're on before editing or committing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds like one of those small habits that prevents big mistakes.

speaker: ALEX
text: It absolutely is.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's get to the heart of review: reading changed files and understanding the diff.

speaker: ALEX
text: When you open a pull request for review, one of the main things you'll inspect is the files changed list. In the PR detail view, you can usually move through the changed files and open one in the Diff Editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the Diff Editor look like conceptually?

speaker: ALEX
text: By default, it's often a split view. Old content on the left, new content on the right. Removed lines appear on the old side, added lines on the new side. That's visually useful, but with a screen reader, raw side-by-side navigation can be tiring.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there another display mode?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Inline view puts the diff into a single flow instead of two side-by-side panes. Some learners prefer that because it reduces lateral complexity. You can toggle between split and inline views in the editor controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But the big recommendation here is still the Accessible Diff Viewer, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For screen reader users, the Accessible Diff Viewer is usually the best way to read changes. Press F7 to move to the next change and Shift+F7 to move to the previous one. Instead of making you navigate every visual artifact in the editor, it announces the change as structured information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give an example of what that means?

speaker: ALEX
text: Imagine the screen reader telling you something like: line changed, one line removed, two lines added, then reading the old and new text in a logical sequence. That is far superior to manually crawling through split columns, where context can be easy to lose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of deciphering a picture, I'm getting a concise explanation of the edit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's why the chapter emphasizes that structured reading is much better than navigating the visual diff manually.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should I be listening for during review?

speaker: ALEX
text: Ask questions like: what changed, why did it change, and does it improve the file? Also: is anything missing, misleading, inaccessible, or inconsistent? If you're reviewing documentation, you might listen for heading hierarchy, clear link text, missing steps, grammar, or formatting problems. If you're reviewing code, the questions shift toward correctness, clarity, safety, tests, and maintainability.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if I notice an issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: Flag it. But flagging doesn't always mean blocking. Some issues are must-fix, some are optional improvements, and some are questions. That distinction matters a lot when you write comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave the extension workflow, there's a part many learners really want: creating a pull request from VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The extension can help you create a pull request from your editor. Before you do that, make sure you have a branch with commits pushed to GitHub. The extension can't create a meaningful PR if your work isn't available on the remote.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the ways to create it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Method one, and generally the recommended one, is from the Command Palette. Search for a GitHub Pull Requests create command. Method two is from the Source Control panel, where VS Code may offer a Create Pull Request action when your branch is pushed and differs from the base branch. Method three is from the GitHub panel itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When the PR form opens, what fields matter most?

speaker: ALEX
text: Title is required. Description is optional in a technical sense, but strongly recommended. Then you choose the base branch, which is the target you're merging into, usually main. The compare branch is your source branch. You may also be able to add reviewers, labels, a milestone, and mark the PR as draft.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack draft for a second.

speaker: ALEX
text: A draft pull request says, in effect, "Please look if you want, but this isn't ready for final decision yet." That's useful when you want early feedback without implying the work is finished.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, any advice on the form itself?

speaker: ALEX
text: Move steadily through the fields, confirm which branch is base and which is compare, and don't rush through the controls because they're easy to invert mentally. Title first, description next, then metadata like reviewers and labels.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How is submission handled?

speaker: ALEX
text: Some flows are interactive and prompt you step by step for title, body, and base branch. Other flows are more inline, where you fill details directly in a form. You may get an option to create as draft, or to open the form in your browser if you prefer or need more stable accessibility for complex fields.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if the in-editor form gets awkward, opening in the browser is a perfectly acceptable move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. The goal is a correct, clear PR, not loyalty to a single interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That leads naturally to pull request description templates. Why do they matter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Templates reduce ambiguity. They remind authors to include the information reviewers need: what changed, why it changed, related issues, testing performed, and any checklist items.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where are those templates usually stored?

speaker: ALEX
text: They're typically stored in the repository, often in a.github directory as a pull request template file. A repo may also offer multiple templates depending on the kind of change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And a typical template might ask for what?

speaker: ALEX
text: A short description, a related issue reference, the type of change, testing notes, a checklist, and screenshots if applicable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For a screen reader user, what's the best workflow when a template opens?

speaker: ALEX
text: Read the prompts first. Then move section by section and replace placeholder text with real content. Don't leave headings like Description or Testing blank if the project expects them. Templates are there to help reviewers, so filling them out thoughtfully is part of accessibility too.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. Now let's get into comments and formal reviews.

speaker: ALEX
text: You can add an inline comment directly on a changed line. In VS Code, position your cursor on the relevant line and run GitHub Pull Requests: Add Comment from the Command Palette. Then type your comment and submit it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes a good inline comment?

speaker: ALEX
text: Be specific. Mention the exact problem or praise. If possible, explain why it matters. And if you're asking for change, make the next step clear.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give some formats?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. A blocking comment might say, "Blocking: this heading skips from level two to level four, which breaks document structure for screen reader navigation. Please change this to a level three heading." A non-blocking note might say, "Nit: consider shortening this sentence for clarity." A question might say, "Question: should this setup step mention logging in first?"

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are not dumping vague reactions like, "This seems off."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Review comments should be actionable whenever possible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if I have several comments to make across the PR?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then use Start Review rather than posting each comment immediately. That lets you batch multiple comments and submit them together with a final verdict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the verdict options are approve, request changes, or comment only.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Approve means the PR is acceptable to merge. Request changes means there are issues that should be fixed before merge. Comment-only means you're adding feedback without a merge verdict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What happens after submission?

speaker: ALEX
text: Your comments and verdict are attached to the PR. The author gets notified. They may reply, push updates, or ask for clarification. If they revise the PR, reviewers often come back to inspect what changed since the last round.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a note that some review actions can happen from other environments too, correct?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can view a PR diff in your terminal with Git tools if that's part of your workflow, though today's focus is browser and VS Code. And the three formal outcomes still map across environments: approve, request changes, or comment without verdict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about merging pull requests from VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can sometimes merge directly from the extension, assuming prerequisites are met. Typically that means the PR is in a mergeable state, required reviews are satisfied, required checks have passed, and you have permission to merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you do it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Either from the PR detail view, where a merge action may be available, or via a Command Palette command for merging the current PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And merge type matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Common merge types are the default merge commit, squash and merge, and sometimes rebase merge depending on repository settings. Which one to use depends on the team's conventions. Squash is often used to keep history cleaner for small feature branches. The default merge strategy preserves the branch history more explicitly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also auto-merge sometimes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If the repository allows it, you may be able to enable auto-merge so the PR merges automatically once required checks pass.

speaker: JAMIE
text: After merging, what's the housekeeping?

speaker: ALEX
text: Switch back to main and pull the latest changes so your local repo reflects the merged work. Then consider deleting the feature branch if it's no longer needed. Some workflows even combine squash and merge with branch deletion as a standard cleanup step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make room for troubleshooting, because these workflows rarely go perfectly the first time.

speaker: ALEX
text: Agreed. If you see "No pull requests found," first check whether you're signed in, in the correct repository, and looking at the right filter, such as All Open instead of a narrower view.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if you get "Could not create pull request"?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually that points to branch state or permissions. Confirm your changes are committed and pushed, make sure the compare branch exists on GitHub, and check that you have rights to create PRs in that repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And "Authentication failed"?

speaker: ALEX
text: Verify that your GitHub browser session is active, then retry sign-in. Sometimes closing and reopening VS Code helps. If the extension seems stuck, reloading the window from the Command Palette can reset the interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about checkout problems?

speaker: ALEX
text: If checkout fails because of permissions or repository restrictions, you can still review read-only on GitHub.com. That's an explicit fallback in the practice guidance. And if the Diff Editor itself feels too hard to navigate, go straight to F7 and use the Accessible Diff Viewer.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's pause and walk through the first guided practice in plain language.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Good. This is the install-and-sign-in one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Open Extensions with Control+Shift+X. Search for GitHub Pull Requests. Choose the extension published by GitHub. Install it. If prompted, sign in to GitHub through the browser OAuth flow. Return to VS Code. Then verify success by checking Explorer for a GitHub section showing pull requests and issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the learner is done when that GitHub section appears and is populated for the repository they're working in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comes the second practice: check out a PR and leave a comment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Open the Command Palette. Run GitHub Pull Requests: Focus on Pull Requests View. Find an open PR that is not your own. Check it out if permissions allow. Open the changed files. Open a changed file in the Diff Editor. Use F7 and Shift+F7 to navigate changes accessibly. Find one specific item to comment on, such as a typo, unclear sentence, missing step, or a genuinely good improvement. Then add a comment with the Add Comment command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if checkout is blocked?

speaker: ALEX
text: Skip local checkout and review in the browser instead. Use the Files changed tab on GitHub.com and post your comment there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What counts as completion evidence for this part?

speaker: ALEX
text: A completion comment stating whether the extension was installed, whether you signed in, which PR you reviewed, whether you posted the comment inline or on GitHub.com, and a one-sentence summary of what your comment was about.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Expected outcomes are practical, not theoretical.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Install and authenticate the extension. Check out or view a PR. Navigate diffs with F7. Post constructive, specific feedback.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone gets stuck, the recovery steps are very concrete: reload window, retry OAuth, switch to All Open, use browser fallback, use F7, search directly for Add Comment, and ask a facilitator for help verifying the panel.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And the deeper lesson here is worth naming: PR tooling multiplies your impact. Reviewing others' work sharpens your own standards and builds trust in a community.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's move from tool use to accessible code review more broadly. Not just extension mechanics, but how to review well with a screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good shift. There are really two environments for code review: GitHub.com in the browser and VS Code with the extension. You should be able to work in both, because sometimes one is more accessible or more efficient than the other for a given task.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before starting that, what prerequisites should a learner mentally check off?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should already know the basics of pull requests, branches, and diff concepts. You should be signed in to GitHub, have access to the repo, and know how to navigate either the GitHub web UI or VS Code at a basic level.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there were learning cards in this part too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Conceptually, those cards reinforce a few repeatable tasks: view the full PR diff in your terminal if needed, inspect PR details like description and checks, check out the PR branch locally for testing, and choose one of the three review actions: approve, request changes, or leave a comment-only review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start in the browser. How do I review on GitHub.com accessibly?

speaker: ALEX
text: The good news is that the modern interface is now the default. Step one is to reach the Files changed tab of the pull request. That's where the actual diff review happens.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step two?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the file tree to orient yourself. Listen for the list of changed files, which tells you scope. Ask: how many files changed, and which ones are likely high value to inspect first?

speaker: JAMIE
text: So before reading the lines, I should understand the map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Step three is navigating between file diffs. Move file by file rather than wandering line by line through the entire page without context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then step four: read a diff.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Pay attention to what each line announces or shows: whether it's context, a removal, or an addition. The goal is to reconstruct the author's change, not just hear isolated lines.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then comes placing a comment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Step five is placing an inline comment on a specific line. In web review, GitHub provides comment controls adjacent to changed lines. You can also place a multi-line comment when the issue spans several lines.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That seems important for accessibility issues that are structural rather than line-local.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so. A heading hierarchy problem, for example, may need you to reference surrounding lines, not just a single word.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's next after commenting?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step six is reading existing comments and threads. Reviews are conversations. Before adding a duplicate comment, check whether another reviewer already raised the same issue and whether the author has responded.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then step seven is submitting the review.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Choose approve, request changes, or comment. Understand the meaning of each. Request changes should be used for issues that must be resolved before merge. Approve means you're satisfied. Comment-only is for feedback that doesn't need to block progress.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And step eight?

speaker: ALEX
text: Re-request review for authors when appropriate, especially after updates. In some workflows the author or maintainer handles that, but knowing the concept matters: review is iterative.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now mirror that in VS Code with the Accessible Diff Viewer.

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, first open the PR for review through the GitHub extension. Then open a changed file. From there, use the Accessible Diff Viewer with F7.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the navigation model there?

speaker: ALEX
text: F7 moves to the next change, Shift+F7 moves back. The viewer presents the change in a structured way so you can understand additions and deletions without wrestling with split panes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what makes that better than the raw diff editor?

speaker: ALEX
text: Less visual clutter, stronger structure, and more reliable screen reader announcements. Instead of scanning for color and alignment you can't use, you're handed the semantic change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you place comments in VS Code too?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Once you identify the relevant changed line, use the extension's Add Comment command. So the workflow becomes: open PR, inspect changed files, use Accessible Diff Viewer to understand the edit, then attach a comment at the right location.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels more controlled than browser review for some learners.

speaker: ALEX
text: Often it is, especially if VS Code is already your primary workspace.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about the content of review comments, because tooling alone doesn't make feedback helpful.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A useful review comment usually has three parts: what you noticed, why it matters, and what action or suggestion follows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we do examples?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. A blocking example for a documentation PR might be: "Blocking: this keyboard shortcuts page lists a command without indicating the Mac equivalent. That makes the instructions incomplete for Mac users. Please add the Command or Option version here."

speaker: JAMIE
text: Clear, specific, and tied to the learner's experience.

speaker: ALEX
text: A non-blocking nit might be: "Nit: this welcome paragraph could be shortened into two sentences for easier reading." Helpful, but not a merge blocker.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And a question example?

speaker: ALEX
text: "Question: should this setup guide mention that the user must be signed into GitHub before running this command?" That's not asserting an error as fact. It's inviting clarification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also mentions prefixes that set expectations.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Prefixes like Blocking, Nit, Question, Suggestion, or Praise help the author quickly understand the weight of your comment. That reduces social ambiguity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How many comments is too many?

speaker: ALEX
text: There's no fixed number. Too many is when you're overwhelming the author with low-value noise or repeating the same point. If you have lots of small issues, look for patterns and combine where appropriate. Prioritize clarity and signal over volume.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about keyboard reference? I know we won't read a giant list, but what should learners remember?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few anchor keys matter a lot. In VS Code: Control+Shift+P for Command Palette, Control+Shift+X for Extensions, Control+Shift+E for Explorer, F7 and Shift+F7 for Accessible Diff navigation, and Control+Shift+G for Source Control. On Mac, substitute Command for Control and Option for Alt-based shortcuts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in the browser?

speaker: ALEX
text: The exact keys depend more on your screen reader and browser, but the principle is to use heading, button, link, and region navigation to move efficiently, especially within the Files changed tab and comment controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's handle some common review scenarios, because that's where learners often hesitate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Great idea.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario one: "I want to verify the PR only changes what it claims."

speaker: ALEX
text: Start with the PR description. Then inspect the changed file list. If the PR says it updates one setup document but also changes unrelated files, that's a signal to investigate. You're checking scope alignment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario two: "I want to find all changes to one specific section."

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the file tree and search strategically. In VS Code, once the branch is checked out locally, normal editor search can help you find a heading or phrase across files. In the browser, navigate by file and scan for the relevant area.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario three: "I agreed, but then the author made more changes. Did anything new appear?"

speaker: ALEX
text: Return to the updated diff and focus on changes since your last review if the interface offers that view. At minimum, reread the changed files and comment threads so you understand what was added or revised after your earlier approval.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario four: "A reviewer left a comment I don't understand."

speaker: ALEX
text: Read the surrounding diff, check whether the comment is blocking or optional, and ask for clarification if needed. Review comments are part of collaboration, not judgment from a mountaintop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There were also structured exercises around this. Can we turn those into an audio walkthrough?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Exercise A is a complete web review. First, navigate to the pull request. Second, read the PR description. Third, go to Files changed. Fourth, if available, activate focus mode or whatever reading mode improves diff clarity for you. Fifth, find a heading hierarchy issue. Sixth, place a blocking review comment on that heading. Seventh, find a link text issue. Eighth, place a comment on the link. Ninth, submit your review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then reflect.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Ask yourself what was easy, what was difficult, and what clues helped you orient inside the diff.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Exercise B shifts to VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Make sure the extension is installed. Open the GitHub Pull Requests extension. Open the Pull Requests sidebar. View the changed files for a PR. Access the Accessible Diff Viewer with F7. Listen to the first change carefully. Find the heading hierarchy issue. Locate the heading line and add an inline comment. Write your accessible diff comment. Then create the GitHub PR comment through the extension.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And again, reflect on the experience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Especially compare how much context you retained in VS Code versus the web interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then Exercise C is the comparison exercise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Gather your data from both review environments. Navigate to the PR or designated place to leave a reflection comment. Write your comparison of browser versus VS Code. Then review your comment, submit it, and validate your learning at the checkpoint.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do that reflection out loud. Browser versus VS Code: how should learners think about preference?

speaker: ALEX
text: Ask which environment made orientation easier, which made comment placement easier, and which made the diff itself easier to understand.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So, specific strengths.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Maybe the browser made overall PR context easier because the discussion, checks, and review form are native there. Maybe VS Code made the actual code or document changes easier because the Accessible Diff Viewer reduced clutter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the real-world impact?

speaker: ALEX
text: In real collaborative work, you don't need a single perfect tool. You need the judgment to choose the environment that best supports the task and your access needs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also introduces GitHub Copilot during code review. This is one of those areas where people can either overtrust it or ignore it completely.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. Copilot can help you understand code changes, summarize a diff, explain an unfamiliar pattern, or suggest questions to investigate. In VS Code, that might happen with an open diff. On GitHub.com, it may assist through integrated features depending on your setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give an example exchange?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. You might ask Copilot, "Explain what changed in this diff and what risk areas I should inspect." Or, "Summarize the functional impact of these additions." That can give you a starting frame.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But not a final verdict.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Copilot has limitations that are critical to know. It can misread intent, miss context outside the diff, sound confident when uncertain, and overlook project-specific requirements. Best practice is to use it as a helper for understanding, not as a substitute for reviewing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So skill first, assistant second.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That phrase actually connects to the final part: the reviewer's craft.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Good reviewing is not just clicking buttons. It's noticing important things, weighing their impact, and communicating clearly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should a reviewer look for?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start with purpose. Does the change do what the PR says it does? Then clarity. Is the code or documentation understandable? Then correctness. Then accessibility. Then consistency with project style. Then testing or evidence. And finally scope: are there unrelated changes that should be split out?

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the three review actions fit into that judgment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Approve when the work is ready. Request changes when there are blockers. Comment-only when you want to contribute feedback without stopping progress.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you write constructive feedback instead of demoralizing feedback?

speaker: ALEX
text: Focus on the work, not the person. Be specific. Explain why. Offer a path forward. Use respectful language. And include positive observations when they're real, not performative.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So something like, "This section is strong because the steps are explicit, but one accessibility issue remains: the link text 'click here' doesn't describe the destination. Please revise it to the page name."

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect example. Clear praise, clear issue, clear next step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there a reviewer checklist we can leave listeners with?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Before submitting a review, ask: Did I understand the PR description? Did I inspect the changed files rather than guessing? Did I verify the scope matches the claim? Did I note any accessibility, correctness, or clarity issues? Are my comments actionable? Did I choose the right verdict? And if I used Copilot or another aid, did I independently verify its suggestions?

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that. Reviewing becomes a learning tool too, doesn't it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Reviewing teaches you to recognize quality. You start noticing patterns in structure, clarity, tests, accessibility, and collaboration. That makes you a stronger author as well as a stronger reviewer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a teaser about a larger accessibility agents review ecosystem.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The idea is that more tools and agents can assist with code review, perhaps by surfacing patterns, checking policies, or helping summarize changes. But the guiding principle remains the same: skill first, agent second.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So agents help, but they do not replace human judgment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A real review flow might include you reading the PR, using accessibility tools to inspect the diff, optionally asking an assistant for a summary or risk checklist, then making your own decision and writing your own review comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can we restate the practical takeaways from the whole lesson?

speaker: ALEX
text: Of course. Install and sign in to the GitHub Pull Requests and Issues extension. Use it to view pull requests, filter them, and open details. Check out a PR branch when local testing or deeper inspection will help. Review changed files in VS Code, and for screen reader users, strongly prefer the Accessible Diff Viewer with F7 and Shift+F7. Create pull requests from VS Code when convenient, but don't hesitate to open the form in the browser if accessibility is better there. Use PR templates fully and thoughtfully. Add inline comments that are specific, respectful, and actionable. Batch comments into a review when needed, then choose approve, request changes, or comment-only. Merge only when prerequisites are satisfied, and clean up afterward by returning to main, pulling, and deleting old branches when appropriate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And on the craft side: understand the change, evaluate its impact, set expectation with comment prefixes, avoid noisy review behavior, and remember that reviewing is part of learning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Also remember your fallback paths. If the extension gives you trouble, reload the window. If authentication fails, re-establish browser sign-in. If checkout isn't allowed, review on GitHub.com. If the visual diff is too chaotic, use the accessible tools designed for this job.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I think that's the reassuring note many learners need: there is almost always another route.

speaker: ALEX
text: There is. Good collaboration isn't about forcing yourself through one interface. It's about using the tools that let you understand the change and communicate effectively.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you're doing the practice work tied to this lesson, your evidence comment should say whether the extension was installed, whether you signed in, which PR you reviewed, whether your comment was inline or on GitHub.com, and what your comment was about.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then close out any related challenge issues if your course setup uses them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. Today's lesson was a big one, but the through-line is simple: make pull requests manageable, make diffs readable, and make feedback constructive.

speaker: ALEX
text: Beautifully said. We'll stop there.
